tool_args.label: MTA Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan with Route Profiles
tool_args.raw_text: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Draft Plan with Route Profiles - Draft Plan 12/01/22

payload.title: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign: Draft Plan with Route Profiles

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign
tool_args.raw_text: The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign is part of the MTA's larger effort to comprehensively modernize New York City's bus network and improve bus service borough by borough.

payload.description: Draft Plan for the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign, proposing a reimagined Brooklyn bus network with routes, stops, and generalized frequencies.

tool_args.label: MTA New York City Transit (MTA NYCT)
tool_args.raw_text: The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign is part of the Metropolitan Transportation Authority's (MTA) larger effort to comprehensively modernize New York City's bus network.

payload.entity_name: Metropolitan Transportation Authority New York City Transit

tool_args.label: NYC Department of Transportation (NYC DOT)
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT is working collaboratively with the MTA to meet bus priority objectives as part of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.

payload.entity_name: New York City Department of Transportation

tool_args.label: New York City Police Department (NYPD)
tool_args.raw_text: We continue to work closely with the New York City Police Department (NYPD) to expand traffic enforcement of bus lanes.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn bus ridership decline 2016-2019
tool_args.raw_text: Bus ridership in Brooklyn fell 14 percent from 2016 to 2019.

tool_args.label: Average bus speed Brooklyn 2019
tool_args.raw_text: In 2019, average bus speeds in Brooklyn were the second lowest of the five boroughs at 7.0 miles per hour (MPH) and had decreased 5 percent since 2014 (7.4 MPH).

tool_args.label: Average bus speed Brooklyn 2014
tool_args.raw_text: average bus speeds...had decreased 5 percent since 2014 (7.4 MPH).

tool_args.label: Bus speed decline 2014-2019 Brooklyn
tool_args.raw_text: average bus speeds in Brooklyn...had decreased 5 percent since 2014 (7.4 MPH to 7.0 MPH).

tool_args.label: Brooklyn bus ridership pandemic drop
tool_args.raw_text: During the height of the pandemic, bus ridership in Brooklyn fell to roughly 50 percent of pre-pandemic levels.

tool_args.label: Customer Journey Time Performance Brooklyn pre-pandemic
tool_args.raw_text: The average Brooklyn bus customer had a Customer Journey Time Performance (CJTP) of 69 percent prior to the pandemic.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn transit dependency
tool_args.raw_text: About 62 percent of Brooklyn residents rely on public transit for their daily commutes and at least 9 percent commute primarily by bus.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn bus commute share
tool_args.raw_text: at least 9 percent commute primarily by bus

tool_args.label: Average stop spacing on Local routes Brooklyn
tool_args.raw_text: With an average of 830 feet between stops on Local routes

tool_args.label: Time saved per removed bus stop
tool_args.raw_text: removing one stop saves about 20 seconds per trip

tool_args.label: Seniors and people with disabilities among Brooklyn bus customers
tool_args.raw_text: Approximately 19 percent of Brooklyn bus customers are seniors or people with disabilities.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn bus customer wait time vs scheduled
tool_args.raw_text: On an average trip, Brooklyn bus customers waited at the bus stop two minutes longer than scheduled

tool_args.label: Brooklyn bus customer excess onboard time
tool_args.raw_text: spent one minute longer than scheduled onboard the bus

tool_args.label: Local Route Type
tool_args.raw_text: The purpose of Local routes is to connect local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. They are shown in a green color. Service frequencies are typically driven by ridership demand. The average distance between stops on Local routes is approximately between 1,000 and 1,320 feet (1/4 mile).

payload.description: Connects local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. Color: green. Average stop spacing: 1,000-1,320 feet (1/4 mile).

tool_args.label: Rush Route Type
tool_args.raw_text: The purpose of Rush routes is to connect quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Shown in purple. These routes pick up locally and then travel as fast as possible to the subway, stopping only at major transfer points and key destinations. Average stop spacing: approximately 1/4 mile (1,320 feet) in local neighborhoods, approximately 1/3 mile or greater on the 'non-stop' portion.

payload.description: Connects quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Color: purple. Local stop spacing: ~1/4 mile. Non-stop portion spacing: ~1/3 mile or greater. More frequent in AM/PM weekday peak periods.

tool_args.label: Limited Route Type
tool_args.raw_text: The purpose of Limited routes is to serve high-ridership, high-density corridors and connect quickly across the city. They combine with Crosstown (SBS) routes to form a high-frequency core network. Shown in red. Average stop spacing: approximately 1/3 of a mile or greater. Frequent all day (between 6:00 AM and 8:00 PM on weekdays).

payload.description: Serves high-ridership, high-density corridors. Color: red. Average stop spacing: ~1/3 mile or greater. Frequent all day 6 AM-8 PM weekdays.

tool_args.label: Crosstown (SBS) Route Type
tool_args.raw_text: The purpose of Crosstown (SBS) routes is to connect across the city as fast as possible between several important destinations, serving high-ridership, high-density corridors. Shown in blue. Widest bus stop spacing. All-day frequent service (6 AM-8 PM weekdays). Combined with Limited routes to form a high-frequency core network. Average stop spacing: approximately a 1/2-mile (2,640 feet).

payload.description: Connects across the city as fast as possible. Color: blue. Widest stop spacing: ~1/2-mile (2,640 feet). All-day frequent service 6 AM-8 PM weekdays.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign Public Launch
tool_args.raw_text: Fall 2019: Public Launch - Open houses, Pop-up on-street outreach, Surveys

payload.description: Public Launch of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign. Open houses, pop-up on-street outreach, surveys held in October and November 2019.

tool_args.label: Existing Conditions Report Publication
tool_args.raw_text: Winter 2020: Data Analysis and Public Outreach - Published Existing Conditions Report

payload.description: Existing Conditions Report released, outlining key metrics such as population, employment, and demographic trends in Brooklyn and analyzing current bus service.

tool_args.label: COVID-19 Pause of Bus Network Redesign
tool_args.raw_text: Spring 2020 to Summer 2021: COVID-19 Pause

payload.description: Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the bus network redesign initiative was paused to protect public health and ensure resources were concentrated on moving essential workers.

tool_args.label: Bus Redesign Restart Announced
tool_args.raw_text: August 2021: Redesign Restart Announced

payload.description: MTA announced many exciting bus initiatives, including the restart of the Bus Network Redesigns.

tool_args.label: Draft Plan Publication
tool_args.raw_text: Spring 2020 to Fall 2022: Redraw Network and Develop Draft Plan - Publishing Draft Plan (December 2022)

payload.description: Draft Plan of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign published.

tool_args.label: Draft Plan Release and Public Outreach
tool_args.raw_text: Winter/Spring 2023: Draft Plan Release and Public Outreach

payload.description: 18 virtual public workshops held (one for each Community District in Brooklyn). Comment portal and Remix interactive mapping tool available for feedback.

tool_args.label: Top 17 Brooklyn Bus Priority Corridors identified by NYC DOT
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT identified 49 corridors to be studied for potential bus priority street improvements, including 17 top ranked corridors... The following corridors, listed alphabetically, ranked highest in the NYC DOT evaluation process: 18 Av / Coney Island Av; Albany Av / Lefferts Av / E New York Av / Pitkin Av; Avenue Z; Bay Pkwy; Church Av; Flatbush Av; Flatlands Av / Glenwood Rd / Cozine Av; Fulton St; Fulton St / Jamaica Av / Pennsylvania Av; Livingston St / Boerum Pl / Joralemon St; Malcolm X Blvd; Marcus Garvey Blvd / Albany Av / Dean St; Nostrand Av; Ralph Av / E 98 St / New Lots Av; Rockaway Av; Throop Av / Flushing Av / Bergen St; Utica Av

tool_args.label: Corridors studied for bus priority
tool_args.raw_text: This process identified 49 corridors to be studied for potential bus priority street improvements, including 17 top ranked corridors.

tool_args.label: Current frequent route counts in Brooklyn
tool_args.raw_text: Currently, 26 routes operate frequently all-day – defined as every 10 minutes or better between 6am and 8pm on weekdays. An additional 19 routes operate every 15 minutes or better. However, this leaves 21 routes in Brooklyn which do not meet this threshold.

payload.raw_value_text: 26 routes every 10 min or better; 19 routes every 15 min or better; 21 routes below threshold
payload.context: Brooklyn bus routes - routes with frequent all-day service (every 10 min or better 6am-8pm weekdays)

tool_args.label: Routes with 15-minute or better frequency
tool_args.raw_text: An additional 19 routes operate every 15 minutes or better.

payload.raw_value_text: 19 routes operate every 15 minutes or better

tool_args.label: Routes below frequency threshold
tool_args.raw_text: 21 routes in Brooklyn which do not meet this threshold and operate at headways greater than 15 minutes during some time periods.

tool_args.label: 24-hour local bus routes in Brooklyn
tool_args.raw_text: Thirty-three local bus routes operate 24 hours a day and cover the majority of the borough.

tool_args.label: Proposed B44 Local - Nostrand/Rogers Avenues
tool_args.raw_text: The proposed B44 would maintain its existing southbound routing. As a Local route, stops would be spaced slightly farther apart than existing to speed up buses and improve reliability. Weekday frequencies would be increased.

payload.service_description: Service between Bedford-Stuyvesant and Sheepshead Bay

tool_args.label: Proposed B1 Local - 86th Street
tool_args.raw_text: The proposed B1 would maintain its existing routing. As a Local route, stops would be spaced slightly farther apart than existing to speed up buses and improve reliability. No frequency or service span changes are being proposed at this time.

payload.service_description: Service between Bay Ridge and Manhattan Beach

tool_args.label: Proposed B3 Local - Avenue U
tool_args.raw_text: The proposed B3 would maintain its existing routing. As a Local route, stops would be spaced slightly farther apart than existing to speed up buses and improve reliability. No frequency or service span changes are being proposed at this time.

payload.service_description: Service between Gravesend and Bergen Beach

tool_args.label: Flatbush Avenue Bus Priority Project
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT is pursuing bus priority and pedestrian safety improvements on Flatbush Avenue between Tillary Street and Avenue V. With over 118,000 daily bus riders on 12 routes pre-pandemic.

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT is pursuing bus priority and pedestrian safety improvements on Flatbush Avenue between Tillary Street and Avenue V. Flatbush Avenue is a bus priority corridor in the NYC Streets Plan and a Vision Zero priority corridor for safety improvements. Bus speeds average 7 MPH across the corridor, and below 5 MPH in Downtown Brooklyn.
payload.statement: Ongoing bus priority work planned for 2023 on Flatbush Avenue

tool_args.label: B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor Capital Projects
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT is pursuing capital projects along the B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor. Capital work will be implemented along Kings Highway, Flatlands Ave, and Pennsylvania Ave.

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT is pursuing capital projects along the B82 SBS South Brooklyn Corridor along Kings Highway, Flatlands Ave, and Pennsylvania Ave. Planned treatments include expanded median bus stops, bus bulbs, bus pads, median tip expansions, median landscaping, and raised crosswalks.
payload.statement: Ongoing capital project work planned for 2023 on B82 SBS corridor

tool_args.label: Livingston Street Bus Priority Upgrades
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT is pursuing options for upgrading the current bus priority on Livingston Street and improving bus speeds and reliability on this important corridor.

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT is pursuing options for upgrading the current bus priority on Livingston Street. Currently has westbound curbside bus lanes and eastbound offset bus lanes. Bus speeds are well below boroughwide averages. Options may include physically separated bus lanes, bus boarding islands, and/or busways.
payload.statement: Ongoing bus priority work planned for 2023 on Livingston Street

tool_args.label: Flatbush Avenue daily bus riders pre-pandemic
tool_args.raw_text: With over 118,000 daily bus riders on 12 routes pre-pandemic

payload.raw_value_text: over 118,000 daily bus riders on 12 routes pre-pandemic

tool_args.label: Flatbush Avenue bus speed
tool_args.raw_text: Bus speeds average 7 MPH across the corridor, and below 5 MPH in Downtown Brooklyn.

payload.raw_value_text: 7 MPH across the corridor, below 5 MPH in Downtown Brooklyn

tool_args.label: Express route labeling change from X to BM
tool_args.raw_text: For express routes in the Draft Plan, we have continued the recent Network Redesign custom and replaced 'X' labels with 'BM' labels. Peak-only Downtown service patterns now have a single-digit number (e.g., BM1), peak-only Midtown service patterns have a number starting with '3' (e.g., BM31), and off-peak Downtown and Midtown 'combination' variants have a 'c' after the number (e.g., BM1c).

payload.claim_text: Express bus routes re-labeled: X labels replaced with BM labels. Peak-only Downtown: single-digit (BM1). Peak-only Midtown: number starting with 3 (BM31). Off-peak combo variants: 'c' suffix (BM1c). Route color coding: orange for Downtown, purple for Midtown, dark green for Midtown via Downtown destinations.
payload.statement: Express route renumbering and color-coding scheme in the Draft Plan

tool_args.label: Number of proposed express routes
tool_args.raw_text: proposing a total of 19 express routes instead of the existing nine routes.

payload.raw_value_text: 19 express routes instead of the existing nine

tool_args.label: Route labeling convention for non-express routes
tool_args.raw_text: For all routes other than express routes in the Draft Plan, we have used existing 'B' or 'Q' labels where possible. If a proposed route looks much like an existing route, we have kept the existing route label. If a proposed route looks too different to assign it an existing label, we have given it a new 'B' or 'Q' number (e.g., the proposed B81).

payload.claim_text: For non-express routes: existing B/Q labels kept where route is similar; new B/Q numbers assigned where too different (e.g., B81). Existing route labels not appearing in the plan may have been retired and replaced by another B or Q label.
payload.statement: Route labeling convention for non-express proposed routes

tool_args.label: NYC Streets Plan bus priority coordination
tool_args.raw_text: The NYC Streets Plan, released in December 2021, seeks to expand the rollout of bus priority street improvements and the improvement of bus stop amenities. NYC DOT is working collaboratively with the MTA to meet those objectives as part of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.

payload.claim_text: NYC DOT identified key Brooklyn corridors where bus priority street treatments can be implemented. Toolkit includes dedicated bus lanes, busways, queue jump intersections, transit signal priority, pedestrian safety elements, and physical accessibility upgrades of bus stops.
payload.statement: NYC Streets Plan coordination with Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign

tool_args.label: OMNY fare capping introduced February 2022
tool_args.raw_text: We introduced fare capping on February 28, 2022. Customers who tap to pay their fares 12 times ($33) during the week with OMNY will automatically receive free, unlimited rides for the rest of the week (Monday to Sunday).

payload.claim_text: OMNY fare capping: Customers who tap 12 times ($33) during the week receive free unlimited rides for the rest of the week (Monday-Sunday). Introduced February 28, 2022.
payload.statement: OMNY fare capping supports the Bus Network Redesign by improving customers' freedom to travel without worrying about extra fares.

tool_args.label: Central Business District Tolling Program
tool_args.raw_text: The implementation of the Central Business District (CBD) Tolling Program, which would charge vehicles that enter or remain in Manhattan's CBD, will be an effective way to reduce congestion.

payload.claim_text: CBD Tolling Program would charge vehicles entering or remaining in Manhattan's CBD. Expected to reduce congestion, encourage alternative transportation, and provide MTA with a new revenue source for capital investment in bus service.
payload.statement: CBD Tolling Program mentioned as supporting bus speed and reliability improvements.

tool_args.label: 2020-2024 MTA Capital Program
tool_args.raw_text: The 2020-2024 MTA Capital Program includes $54.8 billion of investments within the New York City region, many of which will improve bus service and support the Bus Network Redesign.

payload.claim_text: 2020-2024 MTA Capital Program: $54.8 billion in investments. More than 82 percent dedicated to existing core infrastructure. Priorities: upgrade stations and accessibility, invest in new buses and train cars, modernize signals, build megaprojects, maintain bridges/tunnels and other infrastructure.
payload.statement: Capital Program supports the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign

tool_args.label: 2020-2024 Capital Program budget
tool_args.raw_text: $54.8 billion of investments within the New York City region

tool_args.label: Capital Program dedicated to existing infrastructure
tool_args.raw_text: More than 82 percent of the Capital Program is dedicated to the MTA's existing core infrastructure

tool_args.label: Proposed Final Plan and Additional Outreach
tool_args.raw_text: The Proposed Final Plan will be released after completing the Draft Plan outreach process and reviewing public comments and suggestions to inform any additional changes to the bus network.

payload.description: Proposed Final Plan will include draft bus timetables and a Trip Planning tool. Followed by public open houses, presentations to Borough Board, District Service Cabinet, and community boards before implementation.

tool_args.label: Network redesign strategies for frequency
tool_args.raw_text: We have focused frequency improvements on key corridors and in historically disadvantaged neighborhoods, especially in areas far from the subway where residents and employees tend to be more dependent on the bus.

payload.claim_text: Network redesign strategies to increase frequency include: focusing improvements on key corridors and historically disadvantaged neighborhoods far from subway; reinvesting resources from redundancies; reinvesting resources from speeding up buses; consolidating parallel routes onto one street to increase frequency.

tool_args.label: Strategies to decrease travel time
tool_args.raw_text: We have used the following network redesign strategies to decrease travel time: Expand bus priority in conjunction with NYC DOT, Balance bus stop spacing, Develop new patterns of service (Rush, Crosstown/SBS).

payload.claim_text: Strategies to decrease travel time: 1) Expand bus priority with NYC DOT; 2) Balance bus stop spacing; 3) Develop new service patterns (Rush, Crosstown/SBS).

tool_args.label: Automatic Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) initiative
tool_args.raw_text: We have committed to expanding the Automatic Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) initiative, which utilizes cameras to enforce bus lane rules.

payload.claim_text: MTA works with NYPD to expand traffic enforcement of bus lanes. ABLE initiative uses cameras for bus lane enforcement. Working with NYPD and NYC DOT to deploy Traffic Enforcement Agents.

tool_args.label: B44 proposed Weekday AM Peak frequency
tool_args.raw_text: WEEKDAY 24 hours 7 8 7 8 11 (frequencies in minutes for AM Peak: 7, Midday: 8, PM Peak: 7, Early Evening: 8, Late Evening: 11)

payload.context: B44 route Weekday AM Peak peak direction

tool_args.label: B1 proposed Weekday AM Peak frequency
tool_args.raw_text: WEEKDAY 24 hours 6 7 6 10 18 (frequencies in minutes: AM Peak: 6, Midday: 7, PM Peak: 6, Early Evening: 10, Late Evening: 18)

payload.context: B1 route Weekday AM Peak peak direction

tool_args.label: Flatbush Avenue daily bus routes pre-pandemic
tool_args.raw_text: over 118,000 daily bus riders on 12 routes pre-pandemic

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign → Fall 2019 Public Launch
tool_args.raw_text: The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign launched in October 2019.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign → Draft Plan Publication
tool_args.raw_text: Draft Plan published December 2022.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign partners with NYC DOT
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT is working collaboratively with the MTA to meet those objectives as part of the Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign owned by MTA NYCT
tool_args.raw_text: The Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign is part of the MTA's larger effort to modernize New York City's bus network.

tool_args.label: Brooklyn Bus Network Redesign → Draft Plan Public Outreach
tool_args.raw_text: The Draft Plan Release and Public Outreach will include 18 virtual public workshops in Winter/Spring 2023.

tool_args.label: Express Route Type
tool_args.raw_text: The purpose of Express routes is to connect neighborhoods in the outer boroughs to the central business district in Manhattan with a one-seat ride via the highway.

payload.description: Connects outer borough neighborhoods to Manhattan CBD via highway. Uses coach buses, higher fare than Local. Color-coded by destination: orange for Downtown, purple for Midtown, dark green for Midtown via Downtown. Mostly peak-hour service. Average stop spacing: ~1/3 mile (1,760 feet) in local neighborhoods/Manhattan, excluding highway non-stop portion.

tool_args.label: Bus stop balancing approach
tool_args.raw_text: We have found that removing one stop saves about 20 seconds per trip – and potentially more during peak periods.

payload.claim_text: Bus stop balancing: removing one stop saves about 20 seconds per trip (more in peak periods). Average Local stop spacing in Brooklyn is 830 feet. International peer systems range from 1,000 to 1,680 feet. Rationalizing distance preserves high ridership stops and key destinations.
payload.statement: Bus stop balancing approach described in the Draft Plan

tool_args.label: Public survey and outreach methodology
tool_args.raw_text: We surveyed customers both online and in-person, held ten open houses throughout Brooklyn in October and November 2019, and conducted numerous on-street engagement events.

payload.claim_text: Fall 2019 outreach: online and in-person surveys, ten open houses in Brooklyn (Oct-Nov 2019), numerous on-street engagement events. Five customer priorities identified: Frequent Service, Faster Travel, Reliable Service, Better Connections, An Easy Ride.

tool_args.label: OMNY faster boarding
tool_args.raw_text: Tap readers have been installed on all buses to speed up boarding, so buses spend less time waiting at stops. With significant OMNY usage, we will be able to introduce all-door boarding.

payload.claim_text: OMNY tap readers installed on all buses for faster boarding. Significant OMNY usage will enable all-door boarding.

tool_args.raw_text: The chart below shows how the proposed routes relate to the existing routes. Based on your existing route, you can use this chart to find which of the proposed routes applies to you.

tool_args.label: Top ranked bus priority corridors
tool_args.raw_text: This process identified 49 corridors to be studied for potential bus priority street improvements, including 17 top ranked corridors.

tool_args.label: Existing express route count
tool_args.raw_text: the existing nine routes

tool_args.label: Overnight bus network importance
tool_args.raw_text: Thirty-three local bus routes operate 24 hours a day and cover the majority of the borough. With employment in the largest late-shift sectors expected to continue growing quickly in the next decade, the importance of the overnight bus network will only increase.

payload.claim_text: 33 local bus routes operate 24 hours daily covering most of Brooklyn. Late-shift sectors (healthcare, food services, hospitality/leisure) expected to grow, increasing importance of overnight bus network.

tool_args.label: B44 proposed route improvements
tool_args.raw_text: More direct routing, Improved stop spacing, Improved frequency, Priority Corridor

payload.claim_text: B44 proposed routing change: northbound between Flatbush Av and Fulton St, B44 would travel on Rogers Av to match B44 SBS. B44 SBS routing on Rogers Av reduces travel time and improves reliability by utilizing existing bus lanes. Northbound service on New York Av provided by proposed B49. B44 continues to Williamsburg Bridge Plaza overnight when B44 SBS does not operate. Weekday frequencies increased.

tool_args.label: B44 proposed bus connections
tool_args.raw_text: Bus: B1, B3, B4, B5, B6, B6 LTD, B7, B8, B9, B10, B11, B12, B16, B25, B26, B31, B35, B36, B38, B40, B41, B41 XT, B43, B44 SBS, B45, B48, B49, B52, B53, B54, B55, B57, B65, B66, B69, B81, B82, B82 SBS, B100, B103, Q35 | Subway: (listed separately) | LIRR: Nostrand Avenue

payload.claim_text: Proposed B44 connections include bus connections to over 40 routes, subway connections, and LIRR Nostrand Avenue station.

tool_args.label: NYC DOT bus priority corridor selection criteria
tool_args.raw_text: NYC DOT identified bus priority corridors based on: Demand for bus service, Bus performance, Feasibility of implementing new street treatments including traffic levels and street widths, The corridor's role in the transit network, Neighborhood demographics and equity metrics.

payload.claim_text: Criteria for selecting bus priority corridors: 1) Demand for bus service; 2) Bus performance; 3) Feasibility of implementing new street treatments (traffic levels and street widths); 4) Corridor's role in transit network; 5) Neighborhood demographics and equity metrics.

tool_args.raw_text: Midday 9:00 AM-11:59 PM: 7 minutes

tool_args.label: B1 proposed route improvements
tool_args.raw_text: Improved stop spacing

payload.claim_text: B1 would maintain existing routing. As Local route, stops spaced farther apart. No frequency or service span changes proposed. Route improvements: improved stop spacing.

tool_args.label: B3 proposed route improvements
tool_args.raw_text: The proposed B3 would maintain its existing routing. As a Local route, stops would be spaced slightly farther apart. No frequency or service span changes are being proposed at this time.

payload.claim_text: B3 maintains existing routing. Improved stop spacing (640 ft existing to 1,127 ft proposed). No frequency or service span changes.
